claim: Tom Lodziak's five solo serve drills: 10-in-a-row, plastic-cup target, towel for half-long, same serve to multiple targets, and different serves to different targets.
quote: Execute the same serve to an identical target 10 consecutive times. Use a sheet of paper as your target (fold it to reduce size). You should feel pressure increasing as you approach 10 in a row.

claim: Tom Lodziak's structured drills covering serve, receive, third, fourth and fifth balls — each with explicit difficulty progressions.
quote: Place 8 obstacles across opponent's half. Serve to knock all targets off as quickly as possible. Difficulty increase: Use smaller objects instead of cups.

claim: Tom Lodziak's seven third-ball-attack drills, each beginning fixed (feeder returns to a known place) and progressing to randomised feeder returns.
quote: Progression: Feeder adds more backspin; then randomize push positions across forehand side. Always expect your serve to be returned and anticipate likely return positions to execute strong attacks.

claim: Multiball training accelerates development by raising the rate of high-quality reps and removing ball-retrieval downtime; works for beginners through pros.
quote: Multiball increases the quantity (and often the quality) of balls you can receive in a short period of time. There is no time waste picking up the ball as the rally breaks down.

claim: Serve spin direction biases where receivers tend to return the ball, so the serve type itself is the first half of the third-ball plan.
quote: Most beginners try and return serves they just aim generally for the middle of the table. That's the safest area.

claim: Larry Hodges argues that mindless rapid-fire serve practice is wasted; each serve should be visualised, executed, then evaluated against the visualisation.
quote: with the speed and thoughtfulness of firing a machine gun ... and wonder why their serves aren't any good

claim: Ben Larcombe's 'Service Detention' protocol: 15 minutes of focused serve practice every day after coaching, alternating between reverse, sidespin and fast topspin serves.
quote: every single day, after I have finished coaching

claim: Lodziak's regular vs irregular drill distinction — regular drills build technique; irregular drills/matches build match skills like reading and reaction.
quote: Spending too much time doing regular drills, at the expense of other training methods, can be counter-productive.

claim: Eight effective solo practice methods including serve work on any 30-inch table, shadow play, robot training, multiball feeding, and video review.
quote: Serve and receive are the most important shots in the entire game.

claim: Phone + cheap tripod is enough to record yourself; reviewing footage reveals technical drift you can't feel in real-time, and progress is visible over months.
quote: Modern smart phones have great cameras nowadays and you don't need anything too fancy. A cheap tripod that connects to your phone will allow you to get a good angle.

claim: Players consistently misjudge their own technique on video — body height, wrist angle, hip rotation and footwork range almost always differ from what they feel.
quote: Are you doing what you think you're doing? Probably not.

claim: Recommended weekly structure for intermediate (1200–1800 USATT) players: 4–5 sessions of 60–75 min, with 20 min serve & receive and 20 min match play per session; aim for 40% third-ball-attack conversion.
quote: Place a target (towel or ball box) on the table and aim 20 serves at each of 3 zones: wide forehand, wide backhand, and elbow point. Target: 40% third-ball attack conversion rate in practice matches.
